Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 19 of 2024; Suit No 953 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JCA, Woo Bih Li JAD and Kannan Ramesh JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was incorporated in Singapore on 28 June 2011.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd commenced business operations in cargo trading and bunker trading after its incorporation.
The cargo trading business of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd involved back-to-back purchase and sale of fuel oil with delivery made directly by suppliers to customers.
The bunker trading business of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd involved purchase and delivery of bunker fuel ex-wharf in bulk, followed by breaking bulk and selling parcels to customers.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd held a bunker supplier licence issued by the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore for its bunker trading operations.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd held a bunker craft operator licence from the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore to operate a fleet of vessels to receive and deliver bunker fuel.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd obtained financing facilities from Societe Generale, Singapore Branch, referred to as the SocGen Facility, for both cargo and bunker trading businesses.
Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd obtained financing facilities from Malayan Banking Berhad, referred to as the Maybank Facility, for its cargo trading business.

An audit confirmation request addressed to Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd, referred to as the Mercuria ACR, was prepared by Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's auditor and signed by a director to confirm receivables recorded as due from Mercuria as at the 2017 financial year end.

The audited financial statements of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd for the financial year ended 31 December 2017 recorded trade receivables, including from Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd, as not impaired and described the trade debtors as having good payment records and high credit ratings.

On 13 June 2019, the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore conducted an enforcement check on bunker tankers and discovered tampering of the mass flow meter of a bunker tanker chartered by Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.
On 27 June 2019, the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore temporarily suspended Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd's bunker craft operator licence.

On 28 June 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd sent an email to the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore stating that the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence had the effect of severely impacting the company.

On 3 July 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore and the Singapore Police Force stating that the suspension affected the company’s cash flow and ability to function.

On 4 July 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore appealing for the lifting of the suspension and stating that the company was under financial strain as a result of the suspension.

On 24 July 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore stating that the suspension could lead to Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd going into liquidation and could affect confidence of banks, customers and traders in the Singapore bunker industry.

On 17 July 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd signed a confirmation of indebtedness addressed to Malayan Banking Berhad, referred to as one of the Maybank Confirmations, confirming amounts outstanding under the Maybank Facility.

On 24 July 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd signed further confirmations of indebtedness addressed to Malayan Banking Berhad, referred to as Maybank Confirmations, confirming amounts outstanding under the Maybank Facility.

On 13 November 2018, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd exchanged WhatsApp messages with the company’s Chief Financial Officer requesting management accounts and discussing bunker profit and chartering income in the context of a potential acquisition of the bunker business by a Hong Kong listed company.

On 12 August 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd met another director in Hong Kong and was informed that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was unable to pay its debts to its bank creditors and that an application for judicial management would be filed.
On 16 August 2019, an application was filed to place Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd under judicial management.
On 29 August 2019, Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was placed under interim judicial management.
On 4 September 2019, Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was placed under judicial management.
On 25 March 2021, Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was placed in compulsory liquidation.

Between 21 June 2019 and 2 August 2019, Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd made drawdowns on its bank facilities in the total sum of US$146,047,099.60 for cargo trades, referred to as the Cargo Drawdowns.

Between 21 June 2019 and 2 August 2019, Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd made drawdowns on its bank facilities in the total sum of US$10,508,238.71 for bunker trades, referred to as the Bunker Drawdowns.

On 22 August 2019, at a meeting with Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was informed that the company owed almost US$90 million to Societe Generale and US$60 million to Malayan Banking Berhad for cargo trades.

On 22 August 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd sent an email to the Singapore Police Force reporting the company’s debt exposure to Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad and referring to the possibility of fraud in some transactions.

On 22 August 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd learned from the banks that the bank facilities had been used for cargo trading on a back-to-back basis with counterparties including Citus, Legend Six, Mercuria and Sinochem.

On 22 August 2019, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd informed the Singapore Police Force by email that he previously understood the company’s activities as bunkering and that he had been told by the banks that the facilities were extended for cargo trading on a back-to-back basis.

On 27 February 2020, a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd attended an interview with the company’s judicial managers where he described his understanding that the company was in the bunker industry and that bank lines he signed were for bunker-related activities.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and financial events, the company’s exposure on cargo and bunker trading drawdowns under bank facilities is attributable to the conduct and knowledge of its director in relation to the company’s cargo trading operations and financial position.
Party_Details.Rule: A company director is use reasonable diligence in the discharge of office, including taking reasonable steps to place themselves in a position to guide and monitor the management of the company, and when the company is in financial difficulty or close to insolvency, to consider the interests of creditors when authorising transactions.
Party_Details.Application: Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd operated both cargo and bunker trading businesses financed by facilities from Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad. The company drew down substantial sums on these facilities for cargo and bunker trades between June and August 2019, and these drawdowns were not repaid. During this period, the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore suspended the company’s bunker craft operator licence, and the company’s director focused on communications with the regulator and banks to address the impact on the bunker business. The director signed audit-related documents and bank confirmations, and received management accounts and auditor communications that did not indicate impaired receivables at the end of 2017. The director later learned from the banks in August 2019 of large outstanding cargo-related debts and informed the police of possible fraud in some transactions. These pre-dispute events frame the relationship between the company’s financing structure, its trading activities, and the director’s documented understanding of the scope of operations and financial exposure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed the director’s duty of care, skill and diligence and the duty to take into account creditors’ interests in light of the company’s financial position and the nature of the transactions funded by the bank facilities. It accepted that the director was unaware of the cargo trading business during the material period and that this lack of awareness constituted a breach of the duty of care, skill and diligence because a reasonably diligent executive director would have been aware of all significant lines of business. The court examined four main evidential strands: the 22 August 2019 email to the police, the February 2020 interview transcript, the November 2018 WhatsApp conversation about management accounts, and communications following the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence. From these, it inferred that the director had operated on the basis that the company was a bunker-focused entity and did not appreciate that the Maybank facility was dedicated to cargo trading. The court then considered whether three asserted red flags – the Mercuria audit confirmation request, the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, and the Maybank confirmations – should have prompted inquiries that would have led to discovery of the sham cargo trades. It concluded that the Mercuria audit confirmation request was designed to verify the existence and amount of receivables rather than delinquency, that the 2017 audited financial statements and the auditor’s procedures did not indicate impaired receivables or credit risk, and that management and the auditor had not raised delinquency concerns with the director. The court held that the Mercuria confirmation was not, on the evidence, a red flag of delinquency. It further held that the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence was a significant operational event for the bunker business but did not, in itself, constitute a red flag pointing to irregularities in the cargo trading business, particularly given the director’s focus on lifting the suspension as a means of preserving the company’s operations. The Maybank confirmations were treated as acknowledgments of indebtedness to the bank; the court found no basis to treat them as independent red flags of misconduct in cargo trading, noting that they did not on their face identify the underlying trades as problematic. On the creditor-focused duty, the court applied the two-stage framework from Foo Kian Beng v OP3 International Pte Ltd (in liquidation), distinguishing between the company’s objective financial state and the director’s subjective assessment of the company’s best interests. It held that this duty is engaged when a director exercises discretion to cause the company to enter into or continue transactions at a time when the company is in financial distress or insolvency is inevitable. Because the director did not authorise or participate in the specific cargo drawdowns and was unaware of the cargo trading operations, the court concluded that he did not exercise the relevant discretion in relation to those transactions and therefore could not have breached the creditor-focused duty in respect of them. On loss, the court accepted that the company suffered loss in relation to the cargo drawdowns because the underlying transactions were sham trades, resulting in liabilities to the banks without corresponding assets or cash inflows, and thereby worsening the company’s net asset position. However, the court held that the company bore the legal burden of proving that this loss was caused by the director’s breach of the duty of care. It emphasised that causation must be established by a counterfactual analysis: the company had to show, on a balance of probabilities, what would probably have happened if the director had fulfilled his duty, and that in that scenario the loss would have been avoided. Drawing on authorities such as Sunny Metal & Engineering Pte Ltd v Ng Khim Ming Eric, Lexi Holdings v Luqman, and Weavering Capital (UK) Ltd v Dabhia, the court stated that it was necessary to articulate and support a concrete hypothetical sequence of steps the director would have taken, and how those steps would have led to detection of the sham trades and prevention of the cargo drawdowns. The court found that the company did not plead or prove a sufficiently detailed counterfactual. It noted that the fraud in the cargo trading business was deep-seated and had not been detected by the auditors despite their procedures, and that there was no evidential basis to conclude that, had the director been aware of the cargo trading line, he would have uncovered the sham transactions and stopped the drawdowns. The court rejected the notion that causation could be inferred purely as a matter of common sense from the director’s ignorance of the cargo trading business, holding that such an inference improperly assumed the very causal link that the company was required to prove. As a result, while the director’s lack of awareness of the cargo trading business amounted to a breach of the duty of care, the company failed to establish that this breach caused the loss associated with the cargo drawdowns. The court therefore allowed the appeal and set aside the damages award in respect of the cargo drawdowns, leaving the earlier unappealed findings on other aspects of the case undisturbed.

Metadata.Case_Number: Civil Appeal No 19 of 2024; Suit No 953 of 2020
Metadata.Coram: Tay Yong Kwang JCA, Woo Bih Li JAD and Kannan Ramesh JAD
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Appellate Division of the High Court of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Goh Jin Hian

Facts.Text: Goh Jin Hian was a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd from 28 June 2011.
From 28 June 2011, Goh Jin Hian participated in the management of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd as a director.
From around July 2015, Goh Jin Hian transitioned to a role he described as non-executive director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd while remaining on the board.
On 12 August 2019, Goh Jin Hian resigned as a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd.
During the material period, other directors of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd included Cheung Lai Na (Zoe), Cheung Lai Ming (Sara) and Pek Chong Beng, and the Chief Financial Officer was Wallace To.

On or about 7 February 2018, Goh Jin Hian signed an audit confirmation request addressed to Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd prepared by the company’s auditor, listing receivables recorded as due from Mercuria for the 2017 financial year.

In mid-2018, when presented with the audited financial statements of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd for the year ended 31 December 2017, Goh Jin Hian signed the accounts after the auditor and finance staff indicated there were no significant bad debts.

On 13 November 2018, in a WhatsApp conversation with Chief Financial Officer Wallace To, Goh Jin Hian requested management accounts for Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and discussed bunker profit and chartering income in relation to a potential acquisition of the bunker business by a Hong Kong listed company.

On 28 June 2019, following the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, Goh Jin Hian emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore stating that the suspension had severely impacted Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and appealing for reconsideration.

On 3 July 2019, Goh Jin Hian emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore and the Singapore Police Force stating that the suspension affected Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd’s cash flow and ability to function.

On 4 July 2019, Goh Jin Hian emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore stating that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was under financial strain due to the suspension and requesting that the suspension be lifted.

On 24 July 2019, Goh Jin Hian emailed the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore stating that the continued suspension could lead to Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd going into liquidation and could affect confidence in the Singapore bunker industry.
On 17 July 2019, Goh Jin Hian signed a confirmation of indebtedness addressed to Malayan Banking Berhad confirming amounts outstanding under the Maybank Facility.
On 24 July 2019, Goh Jin Hian signed further confirmations of indebtedness addressed to Malayan Banking Berhad confirming amounts outstanding under the Maybank Facility.

In a WhatsApp exchange after the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, Chief Financial Officer Wallace To informed Goh Jin Hian that the auditor had a going concern issue due to the suspension and that the filing date for the 2018 financial statements had been extended to November.

On 12 August 2019 in Hong Kong, Zoe informed Goh Jin Hian that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd was unable to pay its debts to its bank creditors and that she had decided to file an application to place the company under judicial management; Goh Jin Hian resigned as director on the same day.

On 22 August 2019, after meeting Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad, Goh Jin Hian emailed the Singapore Police Force stating that the banks had informed him that Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd owed almost US$90 million to Societe Generale and US$69 million to Malayan Banking Berhad and that the banks had explained the facilities were extended for cargo trading on a back-to-back basis.

In the 22 August 2019 email to the Singapore Police Force, Goh Jin Hian wrote that he had previously understood the company’s activities as bunkering and that he did not understand why buyers were unable to make payments for cargoes if they had received them, and he referred to the possibility that some bills of lading might be false.

On 27 February 2020, during an interview with the judicial managers of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd, Goh Jin Hian stated that his assumption was that the company was in the bunker industry, that the bank lines he signed were for bunker-related activities, and that there were no cargo traders in the Singapore office.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and documented understanding, the director’s lack of awareness of the company’s cargo trading operations and his actions in relation to audit confirmations, bank confirmations, and regulatory communications are connected to the company’s unrepaid cargo and bunker drawdowns under bank facilities.
Party_Details.Rule: A director is use reasonable diligence in overseeing the company’s affairs, including acquiring and maintaining sufficient knowledge of the company’s business to guide and monitor management, and when authorising transactions in circumstances of financial distress or impending insolvency, to consider the interests of creditors when deciding whether the company should enter into or continue such transactions.
Party_Details.Application: From incorporation until his resignation, Goh Jin Hian served as a director of Inter-Pacific Petroleum Pte Ltd and was involved in management decisions, later describing his role as non-executive from around July 2015. The company operated both cargo and bunker trading businesses financed by facilities from Societe Generale and Malayan Banking Berhad. In early 2018, he signed an audit confirmation request to Mercuria Energy Trading Pte Ltd and later signed the 2017 financial statements after the auditor and finance staff indicated there were no significant bad debts, and the accounts recorded trade receivables as not impaired. In November 2018, he requested management accounts and discussed bunker profit and chartering income in the context of a potential acquisition of the bunker business, without recorded reference to cargo trading performance. After the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore suspended the bunker craft operator licence in June 2019, he focused on communications with the regulator and banks to address the impact on the bunker business, describing the suspension as causing financial strain and potential liquidation risk. In July 2019, he signed confirmations of indebtedness to Malayan Banking Berhad acknowledging outstanding amounts under the Maybank Facility. On 12 August 2019, he was informed that the company could not pay its bank debts and that an application for judicial management would be filed, and he resigned as director. On 22 August 2019, he learned from the banks of large outstanding cargo-related debts and that the facilities had been used for back-to-back cargo trading with specified counterparties, and he reported to the police the possibility of fraud in some transactions. In a February 2020 interview, he described his prior understanding that the company was in the bunker industry and that there were no cargo traders in the Singapore office. These events before the dispute, he signed key financial and banking documents, engaged with regulators and banks, and held a documented understanding of the company’s business focus that did not include awareness of the cargo trading operations financed under the Maybank Facility.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted that Goh Jin Hian was subject to the statutory and common law duty to use reasonable diligence in the discharge of his office and, given his level of involvement in management, applied the standard of a reasonably diligent executive director without special expertise. It concluded that he did not acquire or maintain awareness of the company’s cargo trading business, despite its significance and its financing under the Maybank Facility, and that this lack of awareness constituted a breach of the duty of care, skill and diligence. In reaching this conclusion, the court relied on contemporaneous documents and subsequent explanations. The 22 August 2019 email to the Singapore Police Force showed that he first learned from the banks that the facilities were used for back-to-back cargo trading and that he had previously focused on bunkering. The February 2020 interview transcript recorded him distinguishing between bunker operations and cargo trading, stating that his assumption was that the company was in the bunker industry and that there were no cargo traders in the Singapore office. The November 2018 WhatsApp conversation about management accounts showed him discussing only bunker profit and chartering income in the context of a potential acquisition, without reference to cargo trading performance. His communications after the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence focused on the impact on the bunker business and did not mention cargo trading as an alternative revenue source. The court considered and rejected his explanations that these documents reflected only imprecise language or a focus on particular business lines, finding instead that they evidenced a sustained lack of awareness of the cargo trading operations. On the asserted red flags, the court examined the Mercuria audit confirmation request, the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, and the Maybank confirmations. It held that the Mercuria confirmation was part of the auditor’s standard procedure to verify the existence and amount of receivables and that the document itself stated that the receivables were within usual credit terms. The 2017 audited financial statements recorded no impaired receivables and described trade debtors as having good payment records and high credit ratings. The auditor had performed alternative procedures when Mercuria did not respond to the confirmation and had obtained management representations that there were no disputes or significant bad debts. The court concluded that, on this evidence, the Mercuria confirmation did not signal delinquency and was not a red flag that required Goh Jin Hian to initiate independent collection efforts or further investigation. Regarding the suspension of the bunker craft operator licence, the court found that it was a serious operational event for the bunker business and that Goh Jin Hian reasonably focused on negotiating with the Maritime Port Authority of Singapore to lift the suspension. It held that, in the circumstances, it was not unreasonable for him to concentrate on restoring the bunker operations rather than undertaking a comprehensive review of the company’s entire financial position, and that the suspension did not, by itself, point to irregularities in the cargo trading business. As for the Maybank confirmations, the court viewed them as acknowledgments of indebtedness to the bank and found no indication on their face that the underlying trades were irregular. It held that the confirmations did not independently constitute red flags of misconduct in cargo trading and that their connection to the earlier suspension did not, without more, require him to investigate the nature of the underlying transactions. On the creditor-focused duty, the court applied the framework from Foo Kian Beng v OP3 International Pte Ltd (in liquidation), which requires an assessment of the company’s financial state and the director’s subjective intention when authorising transactions. It held that this duty is engaged when a director exercises discretion to cause the company to enter into or continue transactions at a time when the company is in financial distress or insolvency is inevitable. The court found that Goh Jin Hian did not authorise or participate in arranging the specific cargo drawdowns and was unaware of the cargo trading operations and the use of the facilities for those trades at the time the drawdowns occurred. Because he did not exercise the relevant discretion in relation to the cargo drawdowns, the court concluded that he could not have breached the creditor-focused duty in respect of them. On causation, the court held that although his lack of awareness of the cargo trading business amounted to a breach of the duty of care, the company bore the legal burden of proving that this breach caused the loss associated with the cargo drawdowns. It emphasised that causation must be established through a counterfactual analysis: the company had to show what steps he would probably have taken had he fulfilled his duty and how those steps would have led to detection of the sham trades and prevention of the drawdowns. Citing authorities such as Sunny Metal & Engineering Pte Ltd v Ng Khim Ming Eric, Lexi Holdings v Luqman, and Weavering Capital (UK) Ltd v Dabhia, the court stated that it was necessary to construct a detailed hypothetical scenario rather than rely on general assertions or common sense. The court found that the company did not plead or prove a sufficiently specific counterfactual. It noted that the fraud in the cargo trading business had not been detected by the auditors despite their procedures and that there was no evidential basis to conclude that, had Goh Jin Hian been aware of the cargo trading line, he would have uncovered the sham transactions and stopped the drawdowns. The court rejected the approach of inferring causation solely from the fact that he was unaware of the cargo trading business, holding that such an inference assumed the causal link that the company was required to prove. As a result, the court held that the company had not established that his breach of the duty of care caused the loss from the cargo drawdowns. It therefore allowed his appeal, set aside the damages award against him in respect of the cargo drawdowns, and concluded that he was not liable on the claims advanced in the appeal.
